food_subgroup: Deep-fried crispy rice and lentil flour spiral snack

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Murukku. Nearest parent is a deep-fried grain snack. A new concept for South Indian murukku preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Deep-fried rice flour spiral snack, South Indian (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of murukku is approximately 5 medium spirals (approximately 50g) — extremely crispy, golden-brown, hard deep-fried rice flour spirals with a distinctly savoury, cumin-sesame flavour.

nutrient_source.title: Murukku — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not have a standalone murukku entry. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN rice flour and urad dal flour composition data with standard deep-frying oil absorption for hard fried snacks (approximately 25–30g oil per 100g cooked murukku based on comparable chakli/murukku published data).

gi_source: No GI data available in University of Sydney GI Database for murukku. Expected moderate GI from rice flour base but insufficient standard data available for testing. GI and GL not reported.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 213mg per 50g serving — moderate; murukku is a salty snack and the sodium per serving accumulates quickly if multiple servings are consumed.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit murukku to 25–30g (2–3 spirals) per occasion. It is easy to overconsume as a festive snack. Choose reduced-salt varieties where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high caloric density (478 kcal/100g); rice flour base is moderate-high GI; the high fat from deep frying blunts the glycaemic response somewhat.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients should limit murukku to 2–3 spirals (20–25g) on special occasions. The very high caloric density means even small portions contribute significant energy.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried in oil with high omega-6 content; urad dal provides folate which supports immune function; cumin and sesame seeds have anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Limit murukku to occasional festive consumption. The deep-frying method and high omega-6 fat content may exacerbate inflammation with regular consumption. The small standard serving (50g) makes it acceptable as an occasional treat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murukku is a classic South Indian deep-fried snack — a dough of rice flour and urad dal flour seasoned with cumin seeds, sesame seeds, butter, and asafoetida, pressed through a star-shaped mould directly into hot oil and fried until golden-brown and remarkably hard and crunchy. The distinctive spiral or circular shape breaks with a satisfying crunch. It has a savoury, slightly nutty, cumin-sesame flavour and is gluten-free and vegan (when made with oil, not butter). In Singapore, murukku is a staple snack at Deepavali (Diwali) celebrations, at Indian sweet shops year-round, and at hawker centres.
common_user_queries: how many murukku can I eat

confidence_reason: No standalone murukku entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB Foodo. Values estimated from ICMR-NIN rice flour and urad dal flour data with standard deep-frying oil absorption for hard fried snacks. High caloric density from oil absorption is well-established for hard fried snacks.
notes: Contains dairy (butter) when made with butter; vegan and dairy-free when made with oil. Cholesterol approximately 2.5mg per 50g serving when made with butter. GI and GL are null — insufficient published data. Very low water content (~3.5%) as murukku is a dry, hard fried snack. Calorie density is very high — weight-for-weight one of the most energy-dense Indian snacks.
